critic_raw:
The CNN-LSTM hybrid versus logistic regression comparison for readmission prediction suffers from the same core problem common in this literature: the logistic regression baseline receives the identical feature set as the CNN-LSTM, including temporal utilization features (rolling admission counts, time-since-last-admission, visit frequency stability). Logistic regression applied to these features is not purely a 'static' baseline — it is already exploiting temporal signal encoded in manually aggregated features. This means the comparison is not between 'temporal modeling' and 'static modeling,' but between two different ways of consuming temporal information. To test whether the CNN-LSTM's sequential architecture adds value, the design needs a condition where the baseline cannot access temporal features at all, or alternatively a comparison to a simpler sequential model (e.g., a single-layer LSTM) rather than logistic regression.

The 180-day look-back window is defined a priori without justification. For chronic disease patients, a 180-day window may capture relevant clinical history for some conditions (CHF, COPD) but be insufficient for others (slowly progressing cancer, ESRD) where multi-year trajectory matters. The choice of sequence truncation at 180 days directly affects what temporal patterns the CNN-LSTM can learn, yet no sensitivity analysis varies this window. Similarly, the CNN filter sizes (32–64) and LSTM hidden dimensions (128 units) are specified without reporting a hyperparameter search outcome, which may indicate post-hoc selection.

The success criterion of '≥0.03 absolute AUROC improvement' is stated as meaningful but is not grounded in a clinical decision analysis or a power calculation. An AUROC improvement of 0.03 may be statistically significant on 10,000 patients but may represent near-zero clinical utility if the baseline AUROC is already ≥0.80 and the improvement does not translate to better calibration or better sensitivity at the operating threshold. The design conflates statistical significance with clinical meaningfulness without providing the calibration and threshold-level analysis needed to support the latter claim.
defender_raw:
The hybrid CNN-LSTM design for readmission prediction is well-motivated by the structure of the feature set it processes. The combination of 1D CNN (to extract local temporal patterns in visit dynamics) followed by LSTM (to capture long-range dependencies across visits) is a natural architecture for EHR sequences where both local clinical deterioration signals and long-range trajectory patterns matter for predicting readmission. The 180-day look-back window with rolling utilization features at 30, 60, and 90-day granularities reflects explicit temporal reasoning about the multi-scale nature of chronic disease dynamics — short-term deterioration and long-term trajectory are both clinically relevant and encoded at different scales.

The temporal split with class stratification within each window is methodologically sound. The 3-year training / 6-month validation / 18-month test structure allocates the most data to training while ensuring the test set is long enough to capture seasonal variation in readmission risk and hospital census patterns. The class stratification within temporal windows ensures that the known 15-25% readmission rate is preserved in each split, preventing the distortion that pure temporal splitting can cause when readmission rates drift over time due to policy changes or patient population shifts.

The success criterion — a minimum 0.03 absolute AUROC improvement with consistent subgroup performance — is both statistically motivated and practically meaningful. A 0.03 absolute improvement on AUROC is detectable with bootstrap confidence intervals on a test set of 2,500 patients and represents a clinically meaningful change in discrimination at typical operating thresholds. The requirement for consistent subgroup performance across follow-up intensity strata directly tests whether the CNN-LSTM advantage is generalizable across the full patient population or concentrated in the high-utilization patients whose frequent visits create rich training sequences.

Expected calibration error as a secondary metric is correctly identified as essential for clinical deployment: readmission risk scores communicated to clinicians are interpreted as probabilities, and a model with poor calibration would misrepresent risk levels even if its rank ordering is accurate.
all_issues_raised: Logistic regression baseline receives temporal features (rolling admission counts, time since last admission), making the comparison 'two ways of using temporal info' rather than 'temporal vs. static'
180-day look-back window is unjustified and varies in clinical appropriateness across chronic disease types; no sensitivity analysis
Success criterion of ≥0.03 AUROC improvement is not grounded in clinical decision analysis or formal power calculation
CNN and LSTM architecture hyperparameters appear pre-specified without reported search, suggesting possible post-hoc selection
No ablation comparing CNN-LSTM against a simpler LSTM-only or CNN-only model to isolate the contribution of the hybrid architecture
all_issues_adjudicated: Logistic regression baseline receives temporal features (rolling admission counts, time since last admission), making the comparison 'two ways of using temporal info' rather than 'temporal vs. static'
180-day look-back window is unjustified and varies in clinical appropriateness across chronic disease types; no sensitivity analysis
CNN and LSTM architecture hyperparameters appear pre-specified without reported search, suggesting possible post-hoc selection
